sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
कुतस्त्वा कश्मलमिदं विषमे समुपस्थितम् ।
अनार्यजुष्टमस्वर्ग्यमकीर्तिकरमर्जुन ॥ २ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
kutas tvā kaśmalam idaṁ viṣame samupasthitam |
anārya-juṣṭam asvargyam akīrti-karam arjuna || 2 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.002 = MBh_06,024.002). Text body identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: My dear Arjuna, how have these impurities come upon you? They are not at all befitting a man who knows the value of life. They lead not to higher planets but to infamy.
The Supreme Lord said: My dear Arjun, how has this delusion overcome you in this hour of peril? It is not befitting an honorable person. It leads not to the higher abodes, but to disgrace.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Kṛṣṇa and the Supreme Personality of Godhead are identical. Therefore Lord Kṛṣṇa is referred to as Bhagavān throughout the Gītā. Bhagavān is the ultimate in the Absolute Truth.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.copyright_holder: Advaita Ashrama, Kolkata (publisher of Swami Gambhirananda's English translation of Sankara-bhasya). No verbatim is captured because no commentary exists for this verse.
commentaries.notes: Site bhagavad-gita.us states explicitly that Sri Sankaracharya did not comment on this sloka and that his commentary starts from 2.10. Standard Advaita Ashrama (Gambhirananda) edition agrees - Sankara's bhasya begins at BG 2.10.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Krishna's question 'kutas tvā kaśmalam' is rhetorical — the rebuke names Arjuna's collapse as kaśmala (a defilement / impurity / faintness). Shankara reads this as Krishna refusing the legitimacy of Arjuna's grief: it is not coming from knowledge (jñāna) but from delusion (moha). It leads neither to heaven (asvargyam) nor to fame (akīrti-karam) — i.e. it serves no end the warrior himself recognises.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.1 – 2.3 Sanjaya said — Lord said — When Arjuna thus sat, the Lord, opposing his action, said: ‘What is the reason for your misplaced grief? Arise for battle, abandoning this grief, which has arisen in a critical situation, which can come only in men of wrong understanding, which is an obstacle for
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja emphasises that Arjuna's grief is anārya — unworthy of the noble — because it abandons the duty (svadharma) of a kṣatriya at the very moment that duty has become unavoidable. The grief is both world-denying (asvargya) and reputation-destroying (akīrtikara), because the sva-dharma of a warrior, performed without attachment, is itself a path to liberation; abandoning it at viṣama-kāla (the critical hour) is the wrong renunciation.

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: kaśmala has a wide semantic range — physical dirt, mental impurity, moral defilement, faintheartedness. Prabhupada chooses the moral-impurity reading; Mukundananda chooses the cognitive (delusion) reading. The Sanskrit allows both because Krishna is rebuking BOTH the affective collapse and its cognitive root simultaneously. Translators who pick one sense lose the other; the original holds both.

literal_meaning: The Supreme Lord said: From where has this faintheartedness/impurity come upon you at this critical hour, Arjuna? It is unworthy of the noble, not conducive to higher worlds, and produces only disgrace.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna's first words. The teaching opens not with comfort but with sharp diagnosis. Krishna refuses to validate Arjuna's collapse — names it as kaśmala (a stain/weakness), names it as anārya (not befitting a noble), names it as serving none of Arjuna's own stated ends (neither higher worlds nor reputation). The verse is doctrinally important because it establishes the chapter's voice: the senior teacher who, having earned the right, will not soften the diagnosis. Arjuna's grief is treated as a fact about his current state, not as evidence that the state is correct.

remaining_gaps: Shankara verbatim — site states he did not comment on 2.2; bhasya begins at 2.10.
